1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the opening of the great chapter of holiness, a summary of the law to be proclaimed to all the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes many laws here were given before and are here repeated. The witnesses note that Henry marks Moses ordered to deliver the summary to all the congregation, not to Aaron and his sons only, for all were concerned to know their duty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the to all the people — the summary proclaimed to the whole congregation; the not to priests only — for all were concerned to know their duty; the against ignorance — that religion is not of God which makes ignorance its mother.

The puritan expositor (Henry) presses that even in the darker ages of the law, that religion could not be of God which boasted of ignorance as its mother; Moses was to make known God's statutes to all and proclaim them through the camp, that every man might learn his duty.

So the witnesses make the verse the preface to the law of holiness: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the to all the people: the summary proclaimed to the whole congregation. The witnesses note the not to priests only: for all were concerned to know their duty. The witnesses note the against ignorance: that religion is not of God which makes ignorance its mother. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the to all the people: the summary proclaimed to the whole congregation. The expositors observe the not to priests only: for all were concerned to know their duty. The expositors observe the against ignorance: that religion is not of God which makes ignorance its mother. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the to all the people, the not to priests only, the against ignorance, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the law of holiness was published to the whole people — God willing that all should know his statutes, not religion built on ignorance.
2:
"Speak unto all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say unto them, Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy." The witnesses fasten on this as the keynote of the whole chapter and indeed the law — the people called to be holy because their God is holy. The witnesses note that Gill notes that all the ten commandments are included in this section, with various other laws judicial and ceremonial, and that the proselytes are included in the address. The witnesses note that the ground of every following command is the holiness of God himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to all the congregation — the charge laid on the whole people; the ye shall be holy — the sum of the law's demand; the for I am holy — the holiness of God the pattern and ground; the the gospel echo — the Apostle taking up the word, "Be ye holy, for I am holy.".

The witnesses agree this verse is the foundation and motive of all that follows: holiness is required of the whole congregation, and its measure and reason is the holiness of the LORD their God — the very word the Apostle takes up as the abiding rule of God's people (1 Pet. 1:16).

So the witnesses make the verse the call to holiness: The witnesses note the to all the congregation: the charge laid on the whole people. The witnesses note the ye shall be holy: the sum of the law's demand. The witnesses note the for I am holy: the holiness of God the pattern and ground. The witnesses note the the gospel echo: the Apostle taking up the word, "Be ye holy, for I am holy." The expositors observe the to all the congregation: the charge laid on the whole people. The expositors observe the ye shall be holy: the sum of the law's demand. The expositors observe the for I am holy: the holiness of God the pattern and ground. The expositors observe the the gospel echo: the Apostle taking up the word, "Be ye holy, for I am holy." The believer is taught to mark the to all the congregation, the ye shall be holy, the for I am holy, the the gospel echo, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the whole law is summed in this — the people holy because their God is holy, the pattern and motive of all obedience, fulfilled in the saints called to holiness.
3:
"Ye shall fear every man his mother, and his father, and keep my sabbaths: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note two commandments joined — the reverence of parents and the keeping of the sabbaths — the duties of the fifth and fourth words. The witnesses note that Gill refers this to the fifth command, the fear being not servile but filial, joined with love, esteem, reverence, and ready obedience. The witnesses note that the mother is named first, the Jews observing, lest the honour of the mother be the more neglected. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fear parents — the fifth command, filial reverence joined with love; the mother first — named before the father, lest her honour be neglected; the keep my sabbaths — the fourth command, reverence for God's holy days; the I am the LORD — the refrain grounding the duty in God himself.

The witnesses agree the joining of parents and sabbaths sets the duties to man and the duties to God side by side at the head of the holy life: reverence for parents, the nearest of earthly authorities, and reverence for God's holy days; even the heathen, Gill notes, next to honouring God, exhorted to the honour of parents.

So the witnesses make the verse the joined duties: The witnesses note the fear parents: the fifth command, filial reverence joined with love. The witnesses note the mother first: named before the father, lest her honour be neglected. The witnesses note the keep my sabbaths: the fourth command, reverence for God's holy days. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the refrain grounding the duty in God himself. The expositors observe the fear parents: the fifth command, filial reverence joined with love. The expositors observe the mother first: named before the father, lest her honour be neglected. The expositors observe the keep my sabbaths: the fourth command, reverence for God's holy days. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the refrain grounding the duty in God himself. The believer is taught to mark the fear parents, the mother first, the keep my sabbaths, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness begins with reverence for parents and for God's sabbaths — the duties to man and to God set together at the head of the holy life.
4:
"Turn ye not unto idols, nor make to yourselves molten gods: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the charge against idolatry — not to turn to idols nor make molten gods — the substance of the first and second commandments. The witnesses note that Gill reads the idols as "things of nought," nothing in the world, of no worth, value, or use to their devotees. The witnesses note that even to look at an idol with attention, or to have respect to it in the heart, was forbidden. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turn not unto idols — not to look to them, even in the heart; the nor molten gods — not to make gods of metal for yourselves; the idols are nothing — things of nought, of no worth or use; the I am the LORD — the true and living God alone to be worshipped.

The witnesses agree the holy life is grounded in the exclusive worship of the true God: to turn from the living God to idols that are nothing is the greatest folly as well as wickedness, the Apostle declaring an idol is nothing in the world (1 Cor. 8:4).

So the witnesses make the verse against idolatry: The witnesses note the turn not unto idols: not to look to them, even in the heart. The witnesses note the nor molten gods: not to make gods of metal for yourselves. The witnesses note the idols are nothing: things of nought, of no worth or use. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the true and living God alone to be worshipped. The expositors observe the turn not unto idols: not to look to them, even in the heart. The expositors observe the nor molten gods: not to make gods of metal for yourselves. The expositors observe the idols are nothing: things of nought, of no worth or use. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the true and living God alone to be worshipped. The believer is taught to mark the turn not unto idols, the nor molten gods, the idols are nothing, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness rests on the worship of the one true God — the turning to idols, which are nothing, being the height of folly and sin.
5:
"And if ye offer a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD, ye shall offer it at your own will." The witnesses note the law of the peace offering renewed — to be offered freely and rightly, that its holiness be preserved. The witnesses note that Gill takes this of the voluntary peace offering, one of the three sorts: a thanksgiving, a vow, and a freewill offering. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch see here true fidelity to Jehovah shown in the worship, the holiness of the sacrificial flesh strictly preserved. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a freewill offering — offered of a man's own will to the LORD; the fidelity in worship — the holiness of the sacrifice strictly kept; the the renewed command — none of it eaten on the third day; the I am the LORD — the worship governed by God's own rule.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) note the command of Lev. 7:15-18 emphatically repeated, that none of the peace offering be eaten on the third day, transgressors threatened with extermination — the worship of God to be kept holy and not corrupted by men's carelessness.

So the witnesses make the verse the peace offering: The witnesses note the a freewill offering: offered of a man's own will to the LORD. The witnesses note the fidelity in worship: the holiness of the sacrifice strictly kept. The witnesses note the the renewed command: none of it eaten on the third day. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the worship governed by God's own rule. The expositors observe the a freewill offering: offered of a man's own will to the LORD. The expositors observe the fidelity in worship: the holiness of the sacrifice strictly kept. The expositors observe the the renewed command: none of it eaten on the third day. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the worship governed by God's own rule. The believer is taught to mark the a freewill offering, the fidelity in worship, the the renewed command, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that even the freewill offering must be offered by God's rule — fidelity to the LORD shown in keeping his worship holy and undefiled.
6:
"It shall be eaten the same day ye offer it, and on the morrow: and if ought remain until the third day, it shall be burnt in the fire." The witnesses note the limit set on the eating of the peace offering — the same day and the morrow only, the remnant burned. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was best to eat it all the same day, but the remainder might be eaten on the morrow, never longer. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch join vv.6-8 as the renewed command, the third-day flesh to be burned. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the same day — the offering best eaten when offered; the and the morrow — the remainder allowed only one day more; the the third day — what remained to be burned in the fire; the reverence — the holy flesh not treated as common food.

The witnesses agree the limit guarded the sacredness of the holy flesh: what was offered to God was not to be treated as common food, kept and stale, but eaten in its freshness or wholly consumed by fire — the worshipper taught reverence in the very meal of fellowship.

So the witnesses make the verse the time of eating: The witnesses note the the same day: the offering best eaten when offered. The witnesses note the and the morrow: the remainder allowed only one day more. The witnesses note the the third day: what remained to be burned in the fire. The witnesses note the reverence: the holy flesh not treated as common food. The expositors observe the the same day: the offering best eaten when offered. The expositors observe the and the morrow: the remainder allowed only one day more. The expositors observe the the third day: what remained to be burned in the fire. The expositors observe the reverence: the holy flesh not treated as common food. The believer is taught to mark the the same day, the and the morrow, the the third day, the reverence, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the holy flesh was to be eaten in its freshness and the remnant burned — the meal of fellowship kept reverent, not made common.
7:
"And if it be eaten at all on the third day, it is abominable; it shall not be accepted." The witnesses note that the peace offering eaten on the third day was abominable and would not be accepted — the offering polluted by disobedience. The witnesses note that Gill reads "in eating be eaten," any least bit eaten on the third day. The witnesses note that Clarke and Ellicott refer to the fuller statement of the law at Lev. 7:18. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eaten on the third day — the holy flesh kept too long; the it is abominable — the offering become loathsome by disobedience; the not accepted — the worship rejected though begun aright; the the lesson — obedience in the manner of worship as well as the matter.

The witnesses agree the lesson is that an offering, however well begun, is spoiled by being kept and eaten contrary to God's rule: what was holy becomes abominable and unaccepted, teaching that obedience in the manner of worship matters as much as the worship itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejected offering: The witnesses note the eaten on the third day: the holy flesh kept too long. The witnesses note the it is abominable: the offering become loathsome by disobedience. The witnesses note the not accepted: the worship rejected though begun aright. The witnesses note the the lesson: obedience in the manner of worship as well as the matter. The expositors observe the eaten on the third day: the holy flesh kept too long. The expositors observe the it is abominable: the offering become loathsome by disobedience. The expositors observe the not accepted: the worship rejected though begun aright. The expositors observe the the lesson: obedience in the manner of worship as well as the matter. The believer is taught to mark the eaten on the third day, the it is abominable, the not accepted, the the lesson, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that a holy offering is spoiled by disobedience — eaten out of its time it becomes abominable and is not accepted, the manner of worship mattering before God.
8:
"Therefore every one that eateth it shall bear his iniquity, because he hath profaned the hallowed thing of the LORD: and that soul shall be cut off from among his people." The witnesses note the penalty: he who ate the third-day flesh bore his iniquity, having profaned the holy thing, and was cut off. The witnesses note that Gill notes the eater was chargeable with sin, pronounced guilty, and endured the punishment of cutting off. The witnesses note that Ellicott dwells on the formula "cut off from his people," a grave legal sentence of death by excision, to be rendered uniformly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bear his iniquity — the eater charged with guilt; the profaned the hallowed thing — the holy treated as common; the cut off — the grave sentence of excision; the the lesson — how jealously God guards the holiness of his worship.

The witnesses agree the gravity of the sentence measures the gravity of profaning what God has hallowed: to treat the holy thing as common was no light fault but a sin bearing the heaviest penalty, teaching how jealously God guards the holiness of his worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the profaned holy thing: The witnesses note the bear his iniquity: the eater charged with guilt. The witnesses note the profaned the hallowed thing: the holy treated as common. The witnesses note the cut off: the grave sentence of excision. The witnesses note the the lesson: how jealously God guards the holiness of his worship. The expositors observe the bear his iniquity: the eater charged with guilt. The expositors observe the profaned the hallowed thing: the holy treated as common. The expositors observe the cut off: the grave sentence of excision. The expositors observe the the lesson: how jealously God guards the holiness of his worship. The believer is taught to mark the bear his iniquity, the profaned the hallowed thing, the cut off, the the lesson, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that to profane the hallowed thing of the LORD was a sin punished by excision — the heavy penalty measuring how holy God holds his worship.
9:
"And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not wholly reap the corners of thy field, neither shalt thou gather the gleanings of thy harvest." The witnesses note the gleaning law — the corner of the field and the gleanings left unreaped, that the poor might gather. The witnesses note that Gill sets it of the harvest of Canaan, barley or wheat, the corner not to be wholly reaped. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch explain not to reap to the extreme edge, nor gather the ears left, but leave them for the distressed and the foreigner. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the harvest — the reaping of the land's grain; the not the corners — the edge of the field left unreaped; the not the gleanings — the fallen ears left ungathered; the for the poor — the portion reserved for the needy and the stranger.

The witnesses agree this law wove charity into the very labour of harvest: the owner was not to grasp the whole increase but to leave a portion as of right for the poor and the stranger, that the needy might share in the land's bounty — the mercy of God built into the economy of his people (cf. Ruth 2:2).

So the witnesses make the verse the gleaning law: The witnesses note the the harvest: the reaping of the land's grain. The witnesses note the not the corners: the edge of the field left unreaped. The witnesses note the not the gleanings: the fallen ears left ungathered. The witnesses note the for the poor: the portion reserved for the needy and the stranger. The expositors observe the the harvest: the reaping of the land's grain. The expositors observe the not the corners: the edge of the field left unreaped. The expositors observe the not the gleanings: the fallen ears left ungathered. The expositors observe the for the poor: the portion reserved for the needy and the stranger. The believer is taught to mark the the harvest, the not the corners, the not the gleanings, the for the poor, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that charity was woven into the harvest — the corner and the gleanings left as the right of the poor, the mercy of God built into his people's life.
10:
"And thou shalt not glean thy vineyard, neither shalt thou gather every grape of thy vineyard; thou shalt leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the gleaning law extended to vineyard and harvest — the small clusters and fallen grapes left for the poor and stranger. The witnesses note that Gill notes the small "infant" clusters were not to be cut off but left, and if the whole vine were such, it all belonged to the poor. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch include the olive and grape that had fallen, left for the poor and stranger. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the small clusters — the infant grapes left uncut; the the fallen grapes — the gleanings not gathered up; the for the poor and stranger — the portion reserved for the needy; the I am the LORD — the charity grounded in obedience to God.

The witnesses agree the closing refrain "I am the LORD your God" sets the whole charity on its true ground: it was not mere benevolence but obedience to God, who claimed the gleanings as the poor man's portion and made care for the needy a part of his service.

So the witnesses make the verse the vineyard gleaning: The witnesses note the the small clusters: the infant grapes left uncut. The witnesses note the the fallen grapes: the gleanings not gathered up. The witnesses note the for the poor and stranger: the portion reserved for the needy. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the charity grounded in obedience to God. The expositors observe the the small clusters: the infant grapes left uncut. The expositors observe the the fallen grapes: the gleanings not gathered up. The expositors observe the for the poor and stranger: the portion reserved for the needy. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the charity grounded in obedience to God. The believer is taught to mark the the small clusters, the the fallen grapes, the for the poor and stranger, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the vineyard's gleanings belonged to the poor by God's claim — charity made not mere benevolence but obedience to the LORD.
11:
"Ye shall not steal, neither deal falsely, neither lie one to another." The witnesses note three sins of dishonesty forbidden together — stealing, dealing falsely, and lying — the substance of the eighth command and more. The witnesses note that Gill refers it to the eighth command, noting the plural "ye," which takes in not the thief only but him who sees and is silent, who is as the thief. The witnesses note that the laws are linked, depriving the poor of the gleanings being as if one robbed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not steal — the eighth command, against theft; the not deal falsely — against fraud and false dealing; the not lie — truth required toward the neighbour; the the silent partaker — he who sees and is silent is as the thief.

The witnesses agree these knit dishonesty in deed and word into one web: stealing, false dealing, and lying lead one to another, and the holy life requires truth and honesty toward the neighbour in all things — the very sins the Apostle bids the renewed man put off (Eph. 4:25).

So the witnesses make the verse the law of honesty: The witnesses note the not steal: the eighth command, against theft. The witnesses note the not deal falsely: against fraud and false dealing. The witnesses note the not lie: truth required toward the neighbour. The witnesses note the the silent partaker: he who sees and is silent is as the thief. The expositors observe the not steal: the eighth command, against theft. The expositors observe the not deal falsely: against fraud and false dealing. The expositors observe the not lie: truth required toward the neighbour. The expositors observe the the silent partaker: he who sees and is silent is as the thief. The believer is taught to mark the not steal, the not deal falsely, the not lie, the the silent partaker, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness requires honesty in deed and word — not to steal, deal falsely, or lie, the sins the renewed man is to put off.
12:
"And ye shall not swear by my name falsely, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the forbidding of false swearing — the abuse of God's name to confirm a lie, which profanes him. The witnesses note that Gill ties it to the foregoing: stealing leads to false dealing, then to lying, then to false swearing, each drawing on the next. The witnesses note that the law reaches every name and epithet of God, not the proper name only, that none be used to seal a falsehood. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the swear not falsely — god's name not used to confirm a lie; the every name of God — not the proper name only but all his epithets; the profane the name — false swearing a desecration of the holy name; the the chain of sin — theft, fraud, lying, and false oath drawing one another on.

The witnesses agree false swearing is the climax of dishonesty and the direct profaning of the holy name: to call God to witness a lie is to dishonour him in the very act meant to honour truth, a breach of the third command and a desecration of his name (Exod. 20:7).

So the witnesses make the verse against false swearing: The witnesses note the swear not falsely: God's name not used to confirm a lie. The witnesses note the every name of God: not the proper name only but all his epithets. The witnesses note the profane the name: false swearing a desecration of the holy name. The witnesses note the the chain of sin: theft, fraud, lying, and false oath drawing one another on. The expositors observe the swear not falsely: God's name not used to confirm a lie. The expositors observe the every name of God: not the proper name only but all his epithets. The expositors observe the profane the name: false swearing a desecration of the holy name. The expositors observe the the chain of sin: theft, fraud, lying, and false oath drawing one another on. The believer is taught to mark the swear not falsely, the every name of God, the profane the name, the the chain of sin, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that to swear falsely by God's name is to profane him — the climax of dishonesty and a breach of the third command.
13:
"Thou shalt not defraud thy neighbour, neither rob him: the wages of him that is hired shall not abide with thee all night until the morning." The witnesses note two oppressions forbidden — fraud and robbery — and the holding back of the hired man's wages overnight. The witnesses note that Gill spreads out the fraud: not to defraud in buying and selling, in retaining wages, in refusing to return a trust or repay a loan; nor to rob openly by force. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes here oppression by fraud and oppression by violence both forbidden. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not defraud — no fraud in trade, wages, trust, or loan; the not rob — no oppression by open violence; the the hired man's wages — not kept back overnight; the the poor labourer — paid at once, his cry rising to God.

The witnesses agree the special care for the labourer's wages reveals the heart of the law: the poor man who lives day to day must be paid at once, his hire not kept back even a night — a mercy the prophets and the Apostle press, that the cry of the withheld wage rises to God (Jas. 5:4).

So the witnesses make the verse against oppression: The witnesses note the not defraud: no fraud in trade, wages, trust, or loan. The witnesses note the not rob: no oppression by open violence. The witnesses note the the hired man's wages: not kept back overnight. The witnesses note the the poor labourer: paid at once, his cry rising to God. The expositors observe the not defraud: no fraud in trade, wages, trust, or loan. The expositors observe the not rob: no oppression by open violence. The expositors observe the the hired man's wages: not kept back overnight. The expositors observe the the poor labourer: paid at once, his cry rising to God. The believer is taught to mark the not defraud, the not rob, the the hired man's wages, the the poor labourer, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness forbids defrauding and robbing the neighbour, and the labourer's wage withheld even a night — the cry of the poor rising to God (Jas. 5:4).
14:
"Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind, but shalt fear thy God: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the tender law guarding the helpless — the deaf who cannot hear the curse, the blind who cannot see the snare. The witnesses note that Gill marks the meanness and wickedness of cursing the deaf, who cannot hear or reply, and reads it of any absent person equally unable to answer. The witnesses note that the fear of God is set as the guard, since the helpless cannot defend themselves, but God sees and will require it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not curse the deaf — who cannot hear or answer the curse; the not snare the blind — no stumbling-block before those who cannot see; the the absent — any who cannot defend themselves included; the fear thy God — the guard, since God sees what man does not.

The witnesses agree the law reaches the secret cruelty that the world overlooks: to wrong those who cannot resist or even know it — the deaf, the blind, the absent — is a sin the more base because unseen, restrained only by the fear of the God who sees all and avenges the helpless.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of mercy to the helpless: The witnesses note the not curse the deaf: who cannot hear or answer the curse. The witnesses note the not snare the blind: no stumbling-block before those who cannot see. The witnesses note the the absent: any who cannot defend themselves included. The witnesses note the fear thy God: the guard, since God sees what man does not. The expositors observe the not curse the deaf: who cannot hear or answer the curse. The expositors observe the not snare the blind: no stumbling-block before those who cannot see. The expositors observe the the absent: any who cannot defend themselves included. The expositors observe the fear thy God: the guard, since God sees what man does not. The believer is taught to mark the not curse the deaf, the not snare the blind, the the absent, the fear thy God, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness forbids the secret cruelty against those who cannot resist — restrained by the fear of the God who sees and avenges the helpless.
15:
"Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honour the person of the mighty: but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbour." The witnesses note the charge of impartial justice — neither favouring the poor nor honouring the mighty, but judging in righteousness. The witnesses note that Gill applies it to judges and witnesses, that none bear false witness nor pronounce an unrighteous sentence, justifying the wicked or condemning the righteous. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note both partialities forbidden — not to respect the poor out of pity, nor the mighty out of fear or favour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no unrighteousness — no wrong in the administration of justice; the not the poor — justice not bent by pity for the poor; the not the mighty — nor by fear or favour of the great; the in righteousness — the neighbour judged by truth alone.

The witnesses agree the law strikes at both ends of corruption: justice is not bent even by compassion for the poor, much less by deference to the great; the judge stands for God, who is no respecter of persons (Acts 10:34), and must mirror his even-handed righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse impartial justice: The witnesses note the no unrighteousness: no wrong in the administration of justice. The witnesses note the not the poor: justice not bent by pity for the poor. The witnesses note the not the mighty: nor by fear or favour of the great. The witnesses note the in righteousness: the neighbour judged by truth alone. The expositors observe the no unrighteousness: no wrong in the administration of justice. The expositors observe the not the poor: justice not bent by pity for the poor. The expositors observe the not the mighty: nor by fear or favour of the great. The expositors observe the in righteousness: the neighbour judged by truth alone. The believer is taught to mark the no unrighteousness, the not the poor, the not the mighty, the in righteousness, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that justice must be impartial — bent neither by pity for the poor nor deference to the mighty, the judge mirroring the God who is no respecter of persons.
16:
"Thou shalt not go up and down as a talebearer among thy people: neither shalt thou stand against the blood of thy neighbour: I am the LORD." The witnesses note two sins against the neighbour's good name and life — the talebearer who carries slander, and the standing against a neighbour's blood. The witnesses note that Gill traces the word for talebearer to a peddler who goes from house to house, carrying stories to others' hurt to curry favour for himself. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the second clause as not standing idly by the blood of one's neighbour, nor seeking his life by false witness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the talebearer — the slanderer carrying tales house to house; the for his own favour — currying favour to others' hurt; the the neighbour's blood — not standing against his life by false witness; the I am the LORD — the neighbour's name and life under God's eye.

The witnesses agree the tongue and the testimony are bound up together: the slanderer who wanders spreading tales, and the false witness who endangers a man's very life, are alike condemned; the holy life guards the neighbour's name and blood as its own, under the eye of the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse against slander and bloodguilt: The witnesses note the the talebearer: the slanderer carrying tales house to house. The witnesses note the for his own favour: currying favour to others' hurt. The witnesses note the the neighbour's blood: not standing against his life by false witness. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the neighbour's name and life under God's eye. The expositors observe the the talebearer: the slanderer carrying tales house to house. The expositors observe the for his own favour: currying favour to others' hurt. The expositors observe the the neighbour's blood: not standing against his life by false witness. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the neighbour's name and life under God's eye. The believer is taught to mark the the talebearer, the for his own favour, the the neighbour's blood, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness guards the neighbour's name and life — forbidding the slanderer and the false witness alike, under the eye of the LORD.
17:
"Thou shalt not hate thy brother in thine heart: thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbour, and not suffer sin upon him." The witnesses note the law reaching the heart — not to hate the brother inwardly — and the duty of faithful rebuke. The witnesses note that Gill marks that hatred in the heart, though never shown in word or deed, is a breach of the sixth command, and that to let a brother go on in sin without reproof is by interpretation a hatred of him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the positive duty: to rebuke the neighbour and not bear sin on his account by silence. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not hate in the heart — inward hatred a breach of the sixth command; the faithful rebuke — the neighbour to be reproved, not flattered; the not suffer sin upon him — silence in his sin a hidden hatred; the true love — that loves enough to rebuke.

The witnesses agree the verse joins inward love and faithful reproof: true love does not nurse secret hatred, nor does it flatter a brother in his sin, but loves him enough to rebuke him; to be silent while he sins is not kindness but a hidden hatred that suffers his ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse against heart-hatred: The witnesses note the not hate in the heart: inward hatred a breach of the sixth command. The witnesses note the faithful rebuke: the neighbour to be reproved, not flattered. The witnesses note the not suffer sin upon him: silence in his sin a hidden hatred. The witnesses note the true love: that loves enough to rebuke. The expositors observe the not hate in the heart: inward hatred a breach of the sixth command. The expositors observe the faithful rebuke: the neighbour to be reproved, not flattered. The expositors observe the not suffer sin upon him: silence in his sin a hidden hatred. The expositors observe the true love: that loves enough to rebuke. The believer is taught to mark the not hate in the heart, the faithful rebuke, the not suffer sin upon him, the true love, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness forbids hatred even in the heart and requires faithful rebuke — silence in a brother's sin being itself a hidden hatred.
18:
"Thou shalt not avenge, nor bear any grudge against the children of thy people, but thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the LORD." The witnesses fasten on this as the crown of the chapter and the sum of the second table — not to avenge nor bear a grudge, but to love the neighbour as oneself. The witnesses note that Gill explains avenging as returning ill for ill, and illustrates the grudge by the rabbinic example of refusing a tool to one who once refused you. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the positive command over against both — to love the neighbour as thyself, the whole law toward man gathered into one word. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not avenge — not to return ill for ill; the not bear a grudge — not to nurse resentment for a past wrong; the love thy neighbour as thyself — the sum of the whole second table; the the gospel crown — the verse our Lord names the second great commandment.

The witnesses agree this is the verse our Lord and his Apostles take up as the second great commandment, on which hang the law and the prophets: love to the neighbour, the fulfilling of the law (Matt. 22:39; Rom. 13:9; Gal. 5:14; Jas. 2:8) — revenge and grudge being its plain contradiction.

So the witnesses make the verse the great commandment: The witnesses note the not avenge: not to return ill for ill. The witnesses note the not bear a grudge: not to nurse resentment for a past wrong. The witnesses note the love thy neighbour as thyself: the sum of the whole second table. The witnesses note the the gospel crown: the verse our Lord names the second great commandment. The expositors observe the not avenge: not to return ill for ill. The expositors observe the not bear a grudge: not to nurse resentment for a past wrong. The expositors observe the love thy neighbour as thyself: the sum of the whole second table. The expositors observe the the gospel crown: the verse our Lord names the second great commandment. The believer is taught to mark the not avenge, the not bear a grudge, the love thy neighbour as thyself, the the gospel crown, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the sum of the law toward man is love of the neighbour as oneself — revenge and grudge its contradiction, the very word Christ and his Apostles make the second great commandment.
19:
"Ye shall keep my statutes. Thou shalt not let thy cattle gender with a diverse kind: thou shalt not sow thy field with mingled seed: neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee." The witnesses note a turn from moral to positive statutes — the forbidding of mingling kinds in cattle, seed, and cloth. The witnesses note that Gill distinguishes the foregoing moral laws, written on the heart, from these of positive institution, whose reasons are not so plain, the decree of the King. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the prohibition of unnatural mixtures of beast, seed, and garment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep my statutes — the turn to positive ordinances of the King; the cattle — not to gender with a diverse kind; the seed — the field not sown with mingled seed; the garment — no cloth of linen and woollen mingled.

The witnesses agree these laws of separation taught Israel by daily life the great lesson of the chapter — distinction and holiness: a people set apart was not to confound the orders God had distinguished, the unmingled field and dress a constant parable of a people kept unmingled with the nations and their ways.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of unmingled kinds: The witnesses note the keep my statutes: the turn to positive ordinances of the King. The witnesses note the cattle: not to gender with a diverse kind. The witnesses note the seed: the field not sown with mingled seed. The witnesses note the garment: no cloth of linen and woollen mingled. The expositors observe the keep my statutes: the turn to positive ordinances of the King. The expositors observe the cattle: not to gender with a diverse kind. The expositors observe the seed: the field not sown with mingled seed. The expositors observe the garment: no cloth of linen and woollen mingled. The believer is taught to mark the keep my statutes, the cattle, the seed, the garment, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the forbidding of mingled kinds taught Israel separation and holiness — a people set apart not to confound the orders God distinguished.
20:
"And whosoever lieth carnally with a woman, that is a bondmaid, betrothed to an husband, and not at all redeemed... she shall be scourged; they shall not be put to death, because she was not free." The witnesses note the case of the betrothed bondmaid wronged — a graver matter than fornication, yet not capital, because she was not yet free. The witnesses note that Gill notes the man and woman are of age, the case following the law of mingling. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark that even the personal rights of slaves were upheld, the maid not to be degraded to mere property. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the betrothed bondmaid — wronged, yet not free nor redeemed; the not put to death — the penalty tempered by her state; the she scourged — a punishment, the wrong not unpunished; the the slave's dignity — even the bondwoman a person, not property.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) draw out the dignity the law gave even to a slave: she was a person, not a chattel, and the wrong against her was punished, yet the unfinished state of her betrothal and bondage tempered the penalty — a humane balance rare in the ancient world.

So the witnesses make the verse the bondmaid's case: The witnesses note the the betrothed bondmaid: wronged, yet not free nor redeemed. The witnesses note the not put to death: the penalty tempered by her state. The witnesses note the she scourged: a punishment, the wrong not unpunished. The witnesses note the the slave's dignity: even the bondwoman a person, not property. The expositors observe the the betrothed bondmaid: wronged, yet not free nor redeemed. The expositors observe the not put to death: the penalty tempered by her state. The expositors observe the she scourged: a punishment, the wrong not unpunished. The expositors observe the the slave's dignity: even the bondwoman a person, not property. The believer is taught to mark the the betrothed bondmaid, the not put to death, the she scourged, the the slave's dignity, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the law upheld even the slave's personal dignity — the wrong punished, yet tempered by her unfinished bondage, a humane balance rare among the nations.
21:
"And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, even a ram for a trespass offering." The witnesses note the trespass offering required of the man — a ram brought to the door of the tabernacle to make atonement for his sin. The witnesses note that Gill notes the offering was brought to the priest of the LORD to offer for him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.21-22 as the appointed trespass offering, the ram brought to the door for the sin done. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the offering — brought to the LORD for the sin; the to the door — presented at the tabernacle by the priest; the a ram — the appointed trespass offering; the the deeper reckoning — the sin against God still requiring atonement.

The witnesses agree that even where the civil penalty was tempered, the sin against God remained and required atonement: the ram of the trespass offering carried the matter beyond the social wrong to its true reckoning before the LORD, every sin needing the blood that covers it.

So the witnesses make the verse the trespass offering: The witnesses note the the offering: brought to the LORD for the sin. The witnesses note the to the door: presented at the tabernacle by the priest. The witnesses note the a ram: the appointed trespass offering. The witnesses note the the deeper reckoning: the sin against God still requiring atonement. The expositors observe the the offering: brought to the LORD for the sin. The expositors observe the to the door: presented at the tabernacle by the priest. The expositors observe the a ram: the appointed trespass offering. The expositors observe the the deeper reckoning: the sin against God still requiring atonement. The believer is taught to mark the the offering, the to the door, the a ram, the the deeper reckoning, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the sin, though its civil penalty was tempered, still required atonement before God — the ram of the trespass offering carrying the matter to its true reckoning.
22:
"And the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering before the LORD for his sin which he hath done: and the sin which he hath done shall be forgiven him." The witnesses note the atonement made by the priest with the ram, and the promise that the sin shall be forgiven. The witnesses note that Gill sees in the priest's offering the type of the atoning sacrifice of Christ. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the sinner, having offered by the prescribed ritual, was expiated and declared free by the officiating priest. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priest atones — with the ram of the trespass offering; the before the LORD — the sin reckoned and covered; the shall be forgiven — the man declared free by the priest; the the type — of the atonement of Christ that truly forgives.

The witnesses agree the verse holds out the gospel in shadow: sin confessed and brought with the appointed sacrifice is forgiven, the priest pronouncing the man clear — a figure of the atonement of Christ, by whom the believer's sin is truly put away and forgiveness assured (1 John 1:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the atonement and pardon: The witnesses note the the priest atones: with the ram of the trespass offering. The witnesses note the before the LORD: the sin reckoned and covered. The witnesses note the shall be forgiven: the man declared free by the priest. The witnesses note the the type: of the atonement of Christ that truly forgives. The expositors observe the the priest atones: with the ram of the trespass offering. The expositors observe the before the LORD: the sin reckoned and covered. The expositors observe the shall be forgiven: the man declared free by the priest. The expositors observe the the type: of the atonement of Christ that truly forgives. The believer is taught to mark the the priest atones, the before the LORD, the shall be forgiven, the the type, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that atonement made with the appointed sacrifice brought forgiveness — a shadow of the atonement of Christ, by whom the believer's sin is truly put away.
23:
"And when ye shall come into the land, and shall have planted all manner of trees for food, then ye shall count the fruit thereof as uncircumcised: three years shall it be as uncircumcised unto you: it shall not be eaten of." The witnesses note the law of the young fruit tree — its fruit accounted uncircumcised and not eaten for the first three years. The witnesses note that Clarke sees no need to seek a mystical meaning: the fruit of a young tree, not yet mature, is unwholesome, its juices not sufficiently elaborated. The witnesses note that Gill sets it of the land of Canaan, whither they were going. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the planted trees — the orchards of the promised land; the three years uncircumcised — the early fruit not eaten; the the natural reason — the immature fruit poor and unwholesome; the the sacred reserve — the firstfruits owed to God before the increase is enjoyed.

The witnesses divide on whether the law is chiefly natural or sacramental: the Wesleyan reader (Clarke) takes it as simple wisdom, the early fruit being poor and unwholesome; while others read the "uncircumcised" fruit as a sacred reserve, the firstfruits owed to God before the increase is enjoyed — yet all agree it taught Israel patience and the priority of God's claim on the land's yield.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of the young tree: The witnesses note the the planted trees: the orchards of the promised land. The witnesses note the three years uncircumcised: the early fruit not eaten. The witnesses note the the natural reason: the immature fruit poor and unwholesome. The witnesses note the the sacred reserve: the firstfruits owed to God before the increase is enjoyed. The expositors observe the the planted trees: the orchards of the promised land. The expositors observe the three years uncircumcised: the early fruit not eaten. The expositors observe the the natural reason: the immature fruit poor and unwholesome. The expositors observe the the sacred reserve: the firstfruits owed to God before the increase is enjoyed. The believer is taught to mark the the planted trees, the three years uncircumcised, the the natural reason, the the sacred reserve, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the young tree's fruit was reserved three years — whether for nature's sake or God's claim, teaching patience and the priority of the LORD on the land's yield.
24:
"But in the fourth year all the fruit thereof shall be holy to praise the LORD withal." The witnesses note the fourth year's fruit made holy, devoted to the LORD in praise before the owner might use it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fruit was separated and devoted to God, given to the priest or redeemed of him, as the tithe was eaten only at Jerusalem or by redemption. The witnesses note that the fourth-year fruit was offered with praise, the firstfruits of the tree consecrated. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the fruit holy — the fourth-year yield devoted to God; the to praise the LORD — offered with thanksgiving; the given or redeemed — to the priest, or bought back as the tithe; the firstfruits — the increase acknowledged as God's gift.

The witnesses agree the law set God first in the orchard's yield: before ever the planter ate, the first true harvest was the LORD's, given in thanksgiving — the principle of firstfruits, that the increase is acknowledged as God's gift and the best returned to him with praise (cf. Prov. 3:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the holy fourth year: The witnesses note the all the fruit holy: the fourth-year yield devoted to God. The witnesses note the to praise the LORD: offered with thanksgiving. The witnesses note the given or redeemed: to the priest, or bought back as the tithe. The witnesses note the firstfruits: the increase acknowledged as God's gift. The expositors observe the all the fruit holy: the fourth-year yield devoted to God. The expositors observe the to praise the LORD: offered with thanksgiving. The expositors observe the given or redeemed: to the priest, or bought back as the tithe. The expositors observe the firstfruits: the increase acknowledged as God's gift. The believer is taught to mark the all the fruit holy, the to praise the LORD, the given or redeemed, the firstfruits, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the first true harvest of the tree was the LORD's, given with praise — the principle of firstfruits, the increase owned as God's gift and the best returned to him.
25:
"And in the fifth year shall ye eat of the fruit thereof, that it may yield unto you the increase thereof: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note that only in the fifth year was the owner free to eat the fruit, with the promise of increase. The witnesses note that Gill notes that from the fifth year, and so in all succeeding years, the fruit might be freely eaten. The witnesses note that Ellicott presses the promise: so far from being losers by waiting, they would be gainers, the tree yielding abundant increase thereafter. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat the fruit — the owner free at last to enjoy it; the the increase — the promise of abundant yield thereafter; the no loss in waiting — the patient obedient gainers, not losers; the I am the LORD — the increase the gift of the covenant God.

The witnesses agree the closing promise rewards the patient obedience the law required: by waiting through the unripe years and consecrating the fourth, Israel was not impoverished but enriched, God repaying the honour given him with the tree's abundant increase — obedience and trust never the loser before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the fifth year's freedom: The witnesses note the eat the fruit: the owner free at last to enjoy it. The witnesses note the the increase: the promise of abundant yield thereafter. The witnesses note the no loss in waiting: the patient obedient gainers, not losers. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the increase the gift of the covenant God. The expositors observe the eat the fruit: the owner free at last to enjoy it. The expositors observe the the increase: the promise of abundant yield thereafter. The expositors observe the no loss in waiting: the patient obedient gainers, not losers. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the increase the gift of the covenant God. The believer is taught to mark the eat the fruit, the the increase, the no loss in waiting, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that patient obedience was rewarded with abundant increase — Israel not impoverished by waiting but enriched, obedience never the loser before the LORD.
26:
"Ye shall not eat any thing with the blood: neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times." The witnesses note two heathen practices forbidden together — eating with the blood, and the use of enchantment and divination. The witnesses note that Gill reads the eating "with" or "over" the blood variously, some of flesh not rightly cleansed of blood, some of an idolatrous and superstitious custom of the Zabians. The witnesses note that the enchantment and observing of times are the divinations of the nations, forbidden to a holy people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat not with the blood — the flesh not eaten with its blood, nor in heathen rite; the no enchantment — the magic arts of the nations forbidden; the observe not times — no reading of omens and lucky days; the the holy people — trusting the LORD, not the soothsayer.

The witnesses agree the verse binds the law of blood to the renouncing of all occult arts: Israel was to be free both of the heathen's bloody and superstitious eating and of their soothsaying, trusting the LORD alone and not the omens and enchantments by which the nations sought to read or bend the future.

So the witnesses make the verse against blood and divination: The witnesses note the eat not with the blood: the flesh not eaten with its blood, nor in heathen rite. The witnesses note the no enchantment: the magic arts of the nations forbidden. The witnesses note the observe not times: no reading of omens and lucky days. The witnesses note the the holy people: trusting the LORD, not the soothsayer. The expositors observe the eat not with the blood: the flesh not eaten with its blood, nor in heathen rite. The expositors observe the no enchantment: the magic arts of the nations forbidden. The expositors observe the observe not times: no reading of omens and lucky days. The expositors observe the the holy people: trusting the LORD, not the soothsayer. The believer is taught to mark the eat not with the blood, the no enchantment, the observe not times, the the holy people, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness renounces both the bloody heathen rite and all divination — Israel to trust the LORD alone, not the omens and enchantments of the nations.
27:
"Ye shall not round the corners of your heads, neither shalt thou mar the corners of thy beard." The witnesses note the forbidding of certain cuttings of the hair and beard — marks of heathen custom and idolatrous mourning. The witnesses note that Gill describes the rounding as cutting the hair alike at temples, forehead, and behind the ears, as the Arabians did. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Herodotus reports the Arab tribes cut their hair so in honour of their god, the practice an idolatrous rite. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not round the head — the hair not cut in the heathen ring; the not mar the beard — the corners of the beard not disfigured; the the heathen rite — done by the Arabs in honour of their god; the separation — israel marked even in custom as the LORD's.

The witnesses agree these laws of the hair and beard were not arbitrary but separated Israel from the idolatry of the nations: what among the heathen was done in honour of a false god or in superstitious mourning was forbidden to a people whose every custom, even of the body, was to mark them as the LORD's and unlike the nations round about.

So the witnesses make the verse the hair and beard: The witnesses note the not round the head: the hair not cut in the heathen ring. The witnesses note the not mar the beard: the corners of the beard not disfigured. The witnesses note the the heathen rite: done by the Arabs in honour of their god. The witnesses note the separation: Israel marked even in custom as the LORD's. The expositors observe the not round the head: the hair not cut in the heathen ring. The expositors observe the not mar the beard: the corners of the beard not disfigured. The expositors observe the the heathen rite: done by the Arabs in honour of their god. The expositors observe the separation: Israel marked even in custom as the LORD's. The believer is taught to mark the not round the head, the not mar the beard, the the heathen rite, the separation, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that even the cutting of hair and beard was governed, to keep Israel from the idolatrous customs of the nations — a people marked as the LORD's in all things.
28:
"Ye shall not make any cuttings in your flesh for the dead, nor print any marks upon you: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the forbidding of gashing the flesh for the dead and of printing marks — the mourning and idolatrous customs of the nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Amorites, Scythians, Carthaginians, and Romans cut and tore their flesh in mourning, even the Roman women tearing their cheeks until forbidden by law. The witnesses note that the "marks" are the tattooed signs the heathen printed on themselves, often in honour of their gods. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no cuttings for the dead — the flesh not gashed in heathen mourning; the no printed marks — the body not branded with idolatrous signs; the the nations' custom — the Amorites and others tearing their flesh; the I am the LORD — the people to bear his name, not false gods'.

The witnesses agree the law guarded both grief and the body from heathen excess: Israel's mourning was not to be the frantic self-wounding of those who had no hope, nor was the body to be branded with idolatrous marks; the people of the LORD were to bear his name, not the signs of false gods, even in their sorrow (cf. Deut. 14:1).

So the witnesses make the verse against cuttings and marks: The witnesses note the no cuttings for the dead: the flesh not gashed in heathen mourning. The witnesses note the no printed marks: the body not branded with idolatrous signs. The witnesses note the the nations' custom: the Amorites and others tearing their flesh. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the people to bear his name, not false gods'. The expositors observe the no cuttings for the dead: the flesh not gashed in heathen mourning. The expositors observe the no printed marks: the body not branded with idolatrous signs. The expositors observe the the nations' custom: the Amorites and others tearing their flesh. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the people to bear his name, not false gods'. The believer is taught to mark the no cuttings for the dead, the no printed marks, the the nations' custom, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness forbade the gashing and branding of the heathen — Israel's grief and body kept from idolatrous excess, bearing the LORD's name and not the signs of false gods.
29:
"Do not prostitute thy daughter, to cause her to be a whore; lest the land fall to whoredom, and the land become full of wickedness." The witnesses note the forbidding of prostituting a daughter — a heathen abomination practised under the cloak of religion. The witnesses note that Gill refers it to the wicked practice of the Phoenicians and Canaanites, whose women prostituted themselves in the temples of their idols. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch give the reason: lest the land fall to whoredom and become full of wickedness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the prostitute not thy daughter — the father forbidden to sell her to whoredom; the the heathen rite — the temple prostitution of the Canaanites; the lest the land fall — the corruption spreading through all; the full of wickedness — the whole land defiled if it be allowed.

The witnesses agree the law struck at the root of a defilement that, once let in, would spread through the whole land: the father who sold his daughter to such uses opened the door to a corruption that no single household could contain, and so the purity of the people and the land alike was guarded by forbidding it utterly.

So the witnesses make the verse against prostitution: The witnesses note the prostitute not thy daughter: the father forbidden to sell her to whoredom. The witnesses note the the heathen rite: the temple prostitution of the Canaanites. The witnesses note the lest the land fall: the corruption spreading through all. The witnesses note the full of wickedness: the whole land defiled if it be allowed. The expositors observe the prostitute not thy daughter: the father forbidden to sell her to whoredom. The expositors observe the the heathen rite: the temple prostitution of the Canaanites. The expositors observe the lest the land fall: the corruption spreading through all. The expositors observe the full of wickedness: the whole land defiled if it be allowed. The believer is taught to mark the prostitute not thy daughter, the the heathen rite, the lest the land fall, the full of wickedness, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the father was forbidden to prostitute his daughter — a heathen abomination that, once allowed, would defile the whole land, the purity of the people guarded by forbidding it.
30:
"Ye shall keep my sabbaths, and reverence my sanctuary: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the law returning to its chief point — the keeping of the sabbaths and reverence for the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Gill sees in the keeping of the sabbaths the great preservative from the idolatry and filthy practices of the nations. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read here the true method of divine worship summed up — the holy day kept and the sanctuary reverenced. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep my sabbaths — the holy day a preservative from idolatry; the reverence my sanctuary — due awe for the place of God's worship; the the chief point — the true method of divine worship summed; the the fruit — life pervaded by the fear of God where these live.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) draw out the fruit: where the LORD's day is kept holy and reverence for his sanctuary lives in the heart, not only are many sins avoided, but social and domestic life is pervaded by the fear of God and marked by chasteness and propriety — true worship the root of a holy life.

So the witnesses make the verse sabbaths and sanctuary: The witnesses note the keep my sabbaths: the holy day a preservative from idolatry. The witnesses note the reverence my sanctuary: due awe for the place of God's worship. The witnesses note the the chief point: the true method of divine worship summed. The witnesses note the the fruit: life pervaded by the fear of God where these live. The expositors observe the keep my sabbaths: the holy day a preservative from idolatry. The expositors observe the reverence my sanctuary: due awe for the place of God's worship. The expositors observe the the chief point: the true method of divine worship summed. The expositors observe the the fruit: life pervaded by the fear of God where these live. The believer is taught to mark the keep my sabbaths, the reverence my sanctuary, the the chief point, the the fruit, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the keeping of the sabbaths and reverence for the sanctuary are the heart of worship — where they live, life is pervaded by the fear of God and many sins are avoided.
31:
"Regard not them that have familiar spirits, neither seek after wizards, to be defiled by them: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the forbidding of all traffic with mediums and wizards — the consulting of familiar spirits that defiles. The witnesses note that Gill explains the diviners by the word for "bottles," the ventriloquists whose voice seemed to come from the belly, as the Pythian prophetess and the maid with a spirit of divination. The witnesses note that to seek after such was to be defiled by them, a turning from the living God to lying spirits. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the familiar spirits — the mediums and ventriloquist diviners; the seek not wizards — no resort to the soothsayers of the nations; the to be defiled — the consulting a pollution, not a curiosity; the I am the LORD your God — the people to seek their God alone.

The witnesses agree the holy people were to seek their God alone for the knowledge of things to come, not the mediums and wizards of the nations: to consult familiar spirits was not a harmless curiosity but a defilement and a covenant treachery, forsaking the LORD for the powers of darkness (cf. Isa. 8:19).

So the witnesses make the verse against mediums: The witnesses note the familiar spirits: the mediums and ventriloquist diviners. The witnesses note the seek not wizards: no resort to the soothsayers of the nations. The witnesses note the to be defiled: the consulting a pollution, not a curiosity. The witnesses note the I am the LORD your God: the people to seek their God alone. The expositors observe the familiar spirits: the mediums and ventriloquist diviners. The expositors observe the seek not wizards: no resort to the soothsayers of the nations. The expositors observe the to be defiled: the consulting a pollution, not a curiosity. The expositors observe the I am the LORD your God: the people to seek their God alone. The believer is taught to mark the familiar spirits, the seek not wizards, the to be defiled, the I am the LORD your God, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that holiness forbids all traffic with mediums and wizards — the consulting of familiar spirits a defilement and a forsaking of the LORD for lying spirits.
32:
"Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head, and honour the face of the old man, and fear thy God: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the law of honour to the aged — to rise before the grey head and reverence the old, in the fear of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Hebrew tradition that a young man rose when an elder came within four cubits, and sat again when he had passed, to show the honour was for his age. The witnesses note that this honour was reckoned among the heathen also a sacred duty, its neglect a crime. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rise before the hoary head — the young to rise before the grey-haired; the honour the old man — due reverence to age and its wisdom; the the heathen too — reckoned a sacred duty even among the nations; the fear thy God — the duty bound to true piety toward God.

The witnesses agree the reverence of age is bound to the fear of God: to honour the grey head is to honour the wisdom and the Maker that crowned it, and the closing "fear thy God" sets the duty beyond mere manners, making respect for the aged a part of true piety toward the God of all the generations.

So the witnesses make the verse honour to the aged: The witnesses note the rise before the hoary head: the young to rise before the grey-haired. The witnesses note the honour the old man: due reverence to age and its wisdom. The witnesses note the the heathen too: reckoned a sacred duty even among the nations. The witnesses note the fear thy God: the duty bound to true piety toward God. The expositors observe the rise before the hoary head: the young to rise before the grey-haired. The expositors observe the honour the old man: due reverence to age and its wisdom. The expositors observe the the heathen too: reckoned a sacred duty even among the nations. The expositors observe the fear thy God: the duty bound to true piety toward God. The believer is taught to mark the rise before the hoary head, the honour the old man, the the heathen too, the fear thy God, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the reverence of age is part of the fear of God — to honour the grey head being to honour the Maker who crowned it, beyond mere manners.
33:
"And if a stranger sojourn with thee in your land, ye shall not vex him." The witnesses note the law guarding the stranger — the sojourner not to be vexed or oppressed in Israel's land. The witnesses note that Gill understands it of the proselyte of righteousness, or of the gate, or indeed of any stranger providentially cast among them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the command, already given (Exod. 22:21; Exod. 23:9), not to oppress the foreigner. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sojourner — the stranger dwelling in the land; the ye shall not vex him — no oppression of the defenceless outsider; the any stranger — proselyte or any cast among them; the God his defender — the LORD taking up the cause of the friendless.

The witnesses agree the law reached beyond Israel's own to the defenceless outsider: the stranger, without kin or standing to protect him, was the easy prey of the powerful, and so God himself took up his cause, forbidding the people to vex him and making kindness to the foreigner a mark of the holy nation.

So the witnesses make the verse the stranger guarded: The witnesses note the the sojourner: the stranger dwelling in the land. The witnesses note the ye shall not vex him: no oppression of the defenceless outsider. The witnesses note the any stranger: proselyte or any cast among them. The witnesses note the God his defender: the LORD taking up the cause of the friendless. The expositors observe the the sojourner: the stranger dwelling in the land. The expositors observe the ye shall not vex him: no oppression of the defenceless outsider. The expositors observe the any stranger: proselyte or any cast among them. The expositors observe the God his defender: the LORD taking up the cause of the friendless. The believer is taught to mark the the sojourner, the ye shall not vex him, the any stranger, the God his defender, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the stranger was not to be vexed — God taking up the cause of the defenceless outsider, kindness to the foreigner a mark of the holy nation.
34:
"But the stranger that dwelleth with you shall be unto you as one born among you, and thou shalt love him as thyself; for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the high reach of the law — the stranger to be treated as native-born and loved as oneself, on the ground of Israel's own bondage in Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, especially as a proselyte of righteousness, the stranger enjoyed the same civil and religious privileges, one law for both. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch press the command to love him as thyself, remembering they too were strangers in Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as one born among you — the stranger treated as native, one law for both; the love him as thyself — the royal law of love stretched to the foreigner; the ye were strangers in Egypt — the duty grounded in remembered mercy; the I am the LORD — the God who loved them when strangers.

The witnesses agree this is the same royal law of love (v.18) now stretched to the foreigner: the very word "love him as thyself" spoken of the neighbour is here spoken of the stranger, and grounded in remembered mercy — Israel, once a stranger and oppressed, was to deal with the stranger as God had dealt with them, the gospel's breadth foreshadowed in the law.

So the witnesses make the verse love to the stranger: The witnesses note the as one born among you: the stranger treated as native, one law for both. The witnesses note the love him as thyself: the royal law of love stretched to the foreigner. The witnesses note the ye were strangers in Egypt: the duty grounded in remembered mercy. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the God who loved them when strangers. The expositors observe the as one born among you: the stranger treated as native, one law for both. The expositors observe the love him as thyself: the royal law of love stretched to the foreigner. The expositors observe the ye were strangers in Egypt: the duty grounded in remembered mercy. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the God who loved them when strangers. The believer is taught to mark the as one born among you, the love him as thyself, the ye were strangers in Egypt, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the stranger was to be loved as oneself — the royal law of v.18 stretched to the foreigner and grounded in Israel's own remembered bondage, the gospel's breadth foreshadowed.
35:
"Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment, in meteyard, in weight, or in measure." The witnesses note the law of justice carried into trade — no unrighteousness in measuring length, weight, or quantity. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jarchi's remark that a measurer is a judge, and if he deals deceitfully he perverts judgment, an abomination causing many evils. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch join this to the judgment of v.15, the same righteousness required in trade as on the bench. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no unrighteousness — the wrong forbidden in all dealing as in the courtroom; the meteyard — no false measure of length in trade; the weight — no false weight in the balance to cheat the buyer; the measure — no false measure of quantity in dry or liquid goods; the the measurer a judge — that to falsify is to pervert judgment in daily life.

The witnesses agree the law makes honesty in business a matter of justice as solemn as the courtroom: the merchant's yard, weight, and measure are a kind of judgment, and to falsify them is to pervert judgment in daily life — God requiring the same righteousness in the market as in the seat of the judge.

So the witnesses make the verse justice in trade: The witnesses note the no unrighteousness: the wrong forbidden in all dealing as in the courtroom. The witnesses note the meteyard: no false measure of length in trade. The witnesses note the weight: no false weight in the balance to cheat the buyer. The witnesses note the measure: no false measure of quantity in dry or liquid goods. The witnesses note the the measurer a judge: that to falsify is to pervert judgment in daily life. The expositors observe the no unrighteousness: the wrong forbidden in all dealing as in the courtroom. The expositors observe the meteyard: no false measure of length in trade. The expositors observe the weight: no false weight in the balance to cheat the buyer. The expositors observe the measure: no false measure of quantity in dry or liquid goods. The expositors observe the the measurer a judge: that to falsify is to pervert judgment in daily life. The believer is taught to mark the no unrighteousness, the meteyard, the weight, the measure, the the measurer a judge, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that justice reaches into trade — false yard, weight, and measure being a perversion of judgment, God requiring righteousness in the market as on the bench.
36:
"Just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin, shall ye have: I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the positive command for honest measures — just balances, weights, ephah, and hin — grounded in the God who redeemed them. The witnesses note that Gill notes the weights, in the Hebrew "stones," were to be neither lighter nor heavier, not one sort to buy with and another to sell, a false weight being an abomination. The witnesses note that the Jews, to keep the weights honest, were not to make them of metals that might rust and lighten, but of polished stone. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the just balances and weights — neither lighter nor heavier, one for all; the a just ephah and hin — honest measures of dry and liquid; the no double standard — not one weight to buy and another to sell; the the redeeming God — integrity claimed by him who brought them from Egypt.

The witnesses agree the closing words lift commercial honesty to its highest motive: the God who brought them out of Egypt claims their integrity in the smallest dealings, so that the just weight in a man's bag is a confession of the redeeming LORD, and the false weight a denial of him.

So the witnesses make the verse just measures: The witnesses note the just balances and weights: neither lighter nor heavier, one for all. The witnesses note the a just ephah and hin: honest measures of dry and liquid. The witnesses note the no double standard: not one weight to buy and another to sell. The witnesses note the the redeeming God: integrity claimed by him who brought them from Egypt. The expositors observe the just balances and weights: neither lighter nor heavier, one for all. The expositors observe the a just ephah and hin: honest measures of dry and liquid. The expositors observe the no double standard: not one weight to buy and another to sell. The expositors observe the the redeeming God: integrity claimed by him who brought them from Egypt. The believer is taught to mark the just balances and weights, the a just ephah and hin, the no double standard, the the redeeming God, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the LORD requires just balances and measures — the honest weight a confession of the redeeming God, the false weight a denial of him.
37:
"Therefore shall ye observe all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the closing charge of the chapter — to observe and do all the LORD's statutes and judgments, on the ground of his authority. The witnesses note that Gill gathers the statutes (ordinances of God's sovereign will) and judgments (laws of justice and equity), ceremonial, judicial, and moral, all to be observed and done. The witnesses note that the closing "I am the LORD" is the great sanction running through the whole chapter. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all my statutes — the ordinances of God's sovereign will; the all my judgments — the laws of justice and equity; the and do them — not to know only but to obey; the I am the LORD — the authority grounding every law.

The witnesses agree the chapter ends as it began, in the authority and holiness of God: every law, great or small, moral or ceremonial, rests on the same ground — "I am the LORD"; and the call is not merely to know or approve the statutes but to do them, holiness consisting in obedience to the God who is himself holy.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing charge: The witnesses note the all my statutes: the ordinances of God's sovereign will. The witnesses note the all my judgments: the laws of justice and equity. The witnesses note the and do them: not to know only but to obey. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the authority grounding every law. The expositors observe the all my statutes: the ordinances of God's sovereign will. The expositors observe the all my judgments: the laws of justice and equity. The expositors observe the and do them: not to know only but to obey. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the authority grounding every law. The believer is taught to mark the all my statutes, the all my judgments, the and do them, the I am the LORD, and to walk before the LORD who is holy. The verse thus records that the whole law rests on "I am the LORD" and calls for doing, not knowing only — holiness consisting in obedience to the God who is holy.
